instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Life Insurance Plan without a Beneficiary: Who Gets the Payout Amount?
One may assume that one of the standard motives behind buying a life insurance policy may be to safeguard the future of loved ones, in case an unfortunate event occurs. This may especially be the case for the main earning members of their family, who may wish to ensure their dependents live a life of financial well-being even in their absence. To achieve this, it seems naturally imperative that the financial compensation from the life insurance policy should reach the right hands when the policyholder passes away.
This is where the concept of a beneficiary in a life insurance policy comes about. However, there may be a scenario wherein there is a life insurance policy without a beneficiary. How does the claim payout function then? Let’s dive deep into the concept of beneficiaries and what may happen in their absence in a life insurance policy.
What Is a Beneficiary in A Life Insurance Plan?
A beneficiary is any individual or organisation chosen by the policyholder to receive the benefits of the life insurance policy in the event of the life insured’s passing away.
Ideally, the beneficiary is chosen when the policy is purchased. One can choose any individual or an institution depending on the terms and conditions of the policy and the insurance company. Usually, policyholders choose their spouses, children, parents, or other close family members to be the beneficiary of their policy.
However, one may also choose other individuals, such as a business partner or a close friend to be the beneficiary. A financial institution, trust, or charity may also be chosen to receive the benefits of the life insurance plan. A beneficiary may also be often referred to as a life insurance nominee.
It is ideal to inform the individual or institution in question when they are added as a beneficiary/nominee to your policy.
Now that the concept of a beneficiary has been explored above, let us understand what may happen in the case of a life insurance policy without a beneficiary.
What If A Life Insurance Policy Has No Beneficiary?
When the life insured passes away in a manner covered by the policy, the usual workings of life insurance dictate that the sum assured be paid to the beneficiary named in the policy.
However, similar to how there may be no predicting when an unfortunate event may occur with the life insured, there is no saying when a beneficiary, too, may no longer be present due to unforeseen circumstances.
So, for a life insurance policy without a beneficiary, how may the claim payout function?
The answer will depend on the situation or scenario in which the claim is being made.
Broadly, there may be three scenarios that may lead to there being no beneficiary in a life insurance policy. These are:
The beneficiary passes away before the policyholder does
The beneficiary passes away at the same time as the policyholder does
The beneficiary passes away after the policyholder does but before the claim amount is paid
No beneficiary has been assigned in the first place.
Each of these scenarios of having no beneficiary on the life insurance policy may be handled differently and may require a set of procedures to be followed. Let’s examine each of these scenarios in detail to get a better understanding.
What if the beneficiary dies before the policyholder does?
Some insurance companies may tackle this kind of situation by asking the policyholder to name multiple beneficiaries, in the form of a primary beneficiary or a secondary beneficiary. If the primary beneficiary is no more present, the secondary beneficiary may take their place and receive the due benefits.
If the secondary beneficiary, too, is unavailable or is not assigned as per the terms and conditions of the plan, then there may be a case of a life insurance policy without a beneficiary. Here, the sum assured amount may be paid to the policyholder’s estate, i.e., their legal heirs, as stated in their will.
However, there is a possibility that their family may have to wait a long time to receive the proceeds in such a scenario.
What if the beneficiary and the policyholder die at the same time?
In rare situations, there may exist the chance that the beneficiary and the policyholder may pass away at the same time, leaving the life insurance policy without a beneficiary.
For instance, if both the policyholder and the beneficiary are travelling in a car and meet with a serious accident, the situation of life insurance with no beneficiary might have to be considered.
Here, it may be crucial to determine who passed away first to find out who receives the benefits of the life insurance plan.
If the policyholder passes away before the beneficiary, even if the difference of time of passing away may be only a matter of a few minutes, the latter might be deemed as the rightful recipient of the life insurance proceeds. The legal heirs of the beneficiary may receive the sum assured as per the terms and conditions of the insurance company/plan and the current laws.
If the beneficiary passes away minutes before the policyholder does, the latter’s legal heirs may be eligible to receive the proceeds. However, if there is a secondary beneficiary available, the proceeds may go to them.
If there is no way to prove who passed away first and the life insurance policy is without a beneficiary, the authorities may assume that it was the policyholder who survived. Hence, as mentioned earlier, the life insurance sum assured amount may be paid to the policyholder’s legal heirs.
What if the beneficiary dies before the life insurance policy plan's death claim is processed and paid by the insurer?
In such a situation, the life insurance authorities may consider the legal heirs of the beneficiary to be eligible for the life insurance proceeds.
However, what if there is a secondary beneficiary involved in such a situation? Even then, the primary beneficiary’s legal heirs may be the rightful recipients of the life insurance sum assured. This is because the beneficiary could be said to have survived the policyholder’s passing away.
What if no beneficiary has been assigned in the first place? [1]
Though this may not be that common, it might still be possible that no beneficiary has been assigned for the life insurance policy in the first place.
Here, the will of the deceased policyholder may be taken into consideration. The Indian Succession Act of 1925 may be followed, and the death benefit allocated as per the will. A succession certificate may have to be issued by the court and the beneficiaries may have to provide a renunciation of legal proof to the insurer.
In the absence of a will, the life insurance policy may become a part of the legal estate of the deceased individual and the relevant laws may be followed.
Thus, if there is no beneficiary on a life insurance policy, the life insurance proceeds may be paid to the deceased policyholder’s legal heirs. In certain circumstances, however, the proceeds may be transferred to the deceased beneficiary’s legal heirs. The will of the deceased policyholder may also be taken into consideration.
Conclusion
Dealing with an unfortunate event may be a difficult process. The financial compensation from a life insurance plan may be of help in such difficult times for the beneficiaries.
Therefore, as a policyholder, one must be aware of the terms and conditions regarding beneficiaries in their life insurance contract. They should know the course of action that may be taken in the event of a life insurance policy without a beneficiary. It may help them have better peace of mind, knowing that their premiums may be headed in the right direction.
References:
https://www.insurancedekho.com/life-insurance/beneficiary
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions.
